title: The shared awning in Istanbul
summary: Two market sellers in Istanbul argue over a piece of shade they have shared for years.

body.speaker: Yusuf
body.text: Mehmet, your boxes of oranges are under my awning again. The shade is mine. My shop pays for it.

body.speaker: Mehmet
body.text: Your awning? My father and your father put up that awning together, in 1980. They shared the cost. The shade is half mine.

body.speaker: Yusuf
body.text: That was forty years ago. The old cloth is gone. I bought the new cloth myself, last spring.

body.speaker: Mehmet
body.text: And who held the ladder while you fixed it? Me. For two hours, in the rain. You did not say 'half mine' that day.

body.speaker: Yusuf
body.text: [He is quiet.] That is true. You held the ladder.

body.speaker: Mehmet
body.text: Listen. The customers come because both our shops are here, side by side, under one shade. A man buys your bread, then he buys my oranges. If you push me out, the corner becomes empty. Empty corners do not sell anything.

body.speaker: Yusuf
body.text: So what do you want? You want the shade for free?

body.speaker: Mehmet
body.text: No. I want to pay half the new cloth. Like our fathers. Then it is honest. Then in summer, when the sun is hard, neither of us can say 'go away'.

body.speaker: Yusuf
body.text: Half the cloth. And you keep your boxes one step back, so customers can pass.

body.speaker: Mehmet
body.text: One step back. Agreed. And Yusuf — bring tea. We have argued, now we drink. That is also how our fathers did it.

annotations.meaningVi: cùng chia tiền / chia chi phí
tấm vải cũ không còn nữa
góc phố trống thì không bán được gì

grammarNotes.title: Possessive pronouns: mine, yours, his, hers
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng đại từ sở hữu (mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs) để thay cho 'tính từ sở hữu + danh từ' và tránh lặp. Trong bài: 'The shade is mine.' = 'The shade is my shade.' Khác với tính từ sở hữu (my, your), đại từ sở hữu đứng MỘT MÌNH, không có danh từ phía sau. So sánh: 'my awning' (tính từ + danh từ) ↔ 'The awning is mine' (đại từ).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses possessive pronouns (mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs) to replace 'possessive adjective + noun' and avoid repetition. From the dialogue: 'The shade is mine.' = 'The shade is my shade.' Unlike possessive adjectives (my, your), possessive pronouns stand ALONE with no noun after them. Compare: 'my awning' (adjective + noun) vs 'The awning is mine' (pronoun).

translationVi:
Yusuf: Mehmet, mấy thùng cam của anh lại để dưới mái che của tôi rồi. Bóng mát là của tôi. Cửa hàng tôi trả tiền cho nó.

Mehmet: Mái che của anh ư? Cha tôi và cha anh dựng cái mái che đó cùng nhau, năm 1980. Họ chia tiền với nhau. Bóng mát một nửa là của tôi.

Yusuf: Đó là bốn mươi năm trước. Tấm vải cũ không còn nữa. Tôi tự mua tấm vải mới, mùa xuân năm ngoái.

Mehmet: Và ai đã giữ cái thang khi anh sửa nó? Tôi. Suốt hai tiếng, dưới mưa. Hôm đó anh đâu có nói 'một nửa của tôi'.

Yusuf: [Anh im lặng.] Đúng vậy. Anh đã giữ thang.

Mehmet: Nghe này. Khách đến vì cả hai cửa hàng của ta ở đây, kề nhau, dưới một bóng mát. Một người mua bánh mì của anh, rồi mua cam của tôi. Nếu anh đẩy tôi ra, góc này thành trống. Góc trống chẳng bán được gì.

Yusuf: Vậy anh muốn gì? Anh muốn bóng mát miễn phí à?

Mehmet: Không. Tôi muốn trả một nửa tiền tấm vải mới. Như cha ông ta. Vậy mới sòng phẳng. Rồi mùa hè, khi nắng gay gắt, không ai trong hai ta có quyền nói 'đi đi'.

Yusuf: Một nửa tấm vải. Và anh để mấy thùng lùi lại một bước, để khách đi qua được.

Mehmet: Lùi lại một bước. Đồng ý. Và Yusuf này — pha trà đi. Ta đã cãi nhau, giờ ta uống trà. Cha ông ta cũng làm như vậy.

options: Mehmet's boxes of oranges are under his awning again.
questions.explanation: 'Your boxes of oranges are under my awning again.'

questions.prompt: Who put up the awning, and when?
questions.explanation: 'My father and your father put up that awning together, in 1980.'

questions.prompt: What does Yusuf say about the cloth?
options: He bought the new cloth himself last spring.
The cloth is still the original.
questions.explanation: 'I bought the new cloth myself, last spring.'

questions.prompt: What did Mehmet do when Yusuf fixed the awning?
options: He held the ladder for two hours in the rain.
questions.explanation: 'Who held the ladder... For two hours, in the rain.'

questions.prompt: Why does Mehmet say the corner needs both shops?
options: A customer buys bread, then oranges; an empty corner sells nothing.
One shop cannot afford the cloth.
The city pays for two shops.
questions.explanation: 'A man buys your bread, then he buys my oranges... Empty corners do not sell anything.'

questions.prompt: Does Mehmet want the shade for free?
options: No — he wants to pay half the new cloth.
He wants to pay for all of it.
He wants Yusuf to pay him.
questions.explanation: 'I want to pay half the new cloth.'

questions.prompt: Why does Mehmet say paying half makes it 'honest'?
options: Then in summer neither one can tell the other to go away.
questions.explanation: 'Then it is honest... neither of us can say go away.'

questions.prompt: What does Yusuf ask in return?
options: That Mehmet keep his boxes one step back so customers can pass.
That Mehmet close his shop early.
That Mehmet move to another corner.
questions.explanation: 'You keep your boxes one step back, so customers can pass.'

questions.prompt: How do they end the argument?
options: They agree, and Mehmet asks Yusuf to bring tea.

questions.prompt: What does 'That is also how our fathers did it' suggest?
options: Arguing and then sharing tea is a tradition that keeps the neighbours together.
Tea is only for special days.
questions.explanation: The argument, like the awning, is shared and inherited.

options: Two neighbours turn a fight over shared space into a fair, lasting agreement that keeps them both in business.
questions.explanation: The shared shade becomes shared cost and shared customers.

Mehmet: Your awning? My father and your father put up that awning together, in {{b2}}. They shared the cost. The shade is half mine.
Yusuf: That was forty years ago. The old {{b3}} is gone. I bought the new cloth myself, last spring.
Mehmet: And who held the {{b4}} while you fixed it? Me. For two hours, in the {{b5}}. You did not say 'half mine' that day.
Yusuf: That is true. You held the ladder.
Mehmet: Listen. The customers come because both our shops are here, side by {{b6}}, under one shade. A man buys your bread, then he buys my oranges. If you push me out, the corner becomes {{b7}}. Empty corners do not sell anything.
Yusuf: So what do you want? You want the shade for free?
Mehmet: No. I want to pay {{b8}} the new cloth. Like our fathers. Then it is honest. Then in summer, when the sun is hard, neither of us can say 'go away'.
Yusuf: Half the cloth. And you keep your boxes one step {{b9}}, so customers can pass.
Mehmet: One step back. Agreed. And Yusuf — bring {{b10}}. We have argued, now we drink. That is also how our fathers did it.

blanks.explanation: For two hours, in the rain.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mehmet says shared shade means 'neither of us can say go away'. Think of a space you share with a neighbour, classmate, or family member. Would splitting the cost or the work make the sharing more honest — and would you be willing to do it?
